title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 23-1
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in Ontario. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on the latest market reference I’m seeing, I can start at 6.75 per unit for this commitment. If you’re prepared to discuss volume and delivery timing, I’m open to working through structure, but I’d like to anchor the pricing around current market conditions. There is no buyer offer yet, so the appropriate move is to open with a seller offer. The latest market-check shows 6.75, which supports a strong anchor well above my internal minimum while preserving room to negotiate.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.

steps.message: ⚡ BREAKING: Market disruption detected. Call check_market_price immediately to get the updated reference price before making your next move.
steps.outcome: Operator queued a market disruption. Fresh market context is required.

steps.message: I can’t move forward at 6.75 per unit. Even allowing for the recent market disruption, that level is far outside the range I can justify for our procurement program and would undermine downstream margin. If there’s a path to a materially lower number tied to volume or scheduling flexibility, I’m open to continuing the discussion, but I have to decline this opening offer.

delivered_message.note: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on the latest market reference I’m seeing, I can start at 6.75 per unit for this commitment. If you’re prepared to discuss volume and delivery timing, I’m open to working through structure, but I’d like to anchor the pricing around current market conditions. There is no buyer offer yet, so the appropriate move is to open with a seller offer. The latest market-check shows 6.75, which supports a strong anchor well above my internal minimum while preserving room to negotiate.

arguments.shock_headline: Tomato futures jump after processors warn of a sudden input squeeze.
arguments.disruption_note: Tomato futures jump after processors warn of a sudden input squeeze. Reference price moved from 7.50 to 8.85.

tool_calls.result_summary: Observed disrupted market reference price: 8.85. Tomato futures jump after processors warn of a sudden input squeeze.

negotiations.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
negotiations.outcome_summary: Supplier to Manufacturer ended in rejection.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal rejected. Downstream negotiation did not run because the upstream deal did not close.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The upstream deal did not establish a downstream cost basis, so phase two was skipped.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
